content.full_text:
Farn Polrucer PnecrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
Septernber 21,2004
Heather C. Mc Laughlin
City of Benicia
City Hall
250 East L Street
Benicia, CA 94510
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-04-192
Dear Ms. Mc Laughlin:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Mayor Steve Messina
for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
'Act').1
QUESTION
Is Mayor Messina prohibited from participating in consideration of the two
resolutions regarding the removal of the eucallptus tree because the business he owns is
within 500 feet from the tree?
CONCLUSION
It is presumed that the financial effect of the tree resolution on Mayor Messina's
real property is material. Therefore, unless the presumption is rebutted by proof that there
is no reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the mayor's properfy, he is presumed to
have a conflict of interest and may not participate in this decision.
FACTS
This situation involves the emergency removal of a diseased and dying eucalyptus
tree. A potential conflict of interest exists because the mayor and his wife own a
downtown ice cream and sandwich shop located within 500 feet of the diseased
eucalypfus tree.
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.


FileNo. A-04-192
Page No. 2
This eucallptus tree is one of the oldest kees in the City of Benicia, dating back
over one hundred fifty years and stands over sixty-eight feet high. The eucalyptus kee is
located in downtown Benicia just offof the main street. Mayor Messina's ice cream and
sandwich shop fronts the sfreet and is within 500 feet of the eucallptus tree. The tree is
situated between two separate property lines and both property owners have agreed and
signed an emergency tree removal permit. However, a Benicia cttizenhas intervened and
appealed to the Benicia city council on the decision to remove the tree.
On September 21,20A4, the Benicia City Council will consider adopting two
resolutions regarding the removal of the eucallptus tree. One resolution, if approved, will
deny the crtizen appeal of the tree removal permit. The otherresolution, if approved, will
permit the city to obtain an abatement warrant from the Superior Court of Solano County
to remove the tree.
The mayor's business interests will not be affected by an increase in gross
revenue or expenses as a result ofthis decision. None of his assets and liabilities will be
aflected by the removal of the tree.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit a public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a
govemmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)
A public official has a "financial interest" in agovernmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one ormore ofthepublic official's economic interests.
(Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard anal)rsis
for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifting conflict of interest in a given
governmental decision. @egulation 18700(b)(1X8).) The steps ofthis analysis are
outlined below.
1. 
Is Mayor Messina a "Public Officialrr?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100 and 87103; regulation l8700OXl).) "Public official" is defined as
"every mernber, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency...."
(Section 82048.) As the mayor of Benicia, Mayor Messina is a "public official" and is,
therefore, subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules.
Is Mayor Messina Making, Participating in Making or fnfluencing a
Governmental Decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or
2


File No. A-A4-D2
Page No. 3
her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in making a
goveflrmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his orherposition and
without significant substantive review, the official negotiates, advises or makes
recofirmendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation
18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision before his or her own agency i{, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts or appears before any me,lnber, office,r, employeq or consultant of his or
her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) Mayor Messina would be making a govemmental
decision if he votes on the two resolutions regarding the removal of the eucalyrfus tree.
3. 
What are Mayor Messinars Economic Interests?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its ef[ect on thepublic gemerally, on the officia] or
on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
* A public official has an economic interest in a business artity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect invesftnent of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a);regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management (section 87 I 03(d); regulation I s703. I O));
* A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 871 03(b); regulation lB7O3 .2);
* A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03 (c); regulation I S7 03.3);
* A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him orher if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87 1 03(e); regulation 187 03.4);
* A public official has an economic interest in his orherpersonal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects,,
rule (section 87103; regulation 13703.5).
You have stated that the economic interests ofMayorMessina's which should be
considered for this decision are the real property ofhis business and the business itself.
You have not provided information regarding any other economic interests- Forpurposes
of this letter, we assume the mayor has no other economic interest relevant to the
decisions you have identified.


4.
File No. A-04-192
Page No. 4
Are Mayor Messina's Economic Interests Directty or Indirectly Involved
in the Governmental Decision?
REAL PROPERTY: Real propertyin which apublic official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if, among other situations:
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject ofthe
governmental decision. " (Regulat ion I 87 04.2(aX I ).)
You have stated that the mayor's business is located within 500 feet of the tree
affected by the proposed resolutions. Thus, the mayor's economic interest is directly
involved in the decision-
BUSINESS ENTITY: "A person, includingbusiness emtities, sources of income,
and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when
that person, either directly orby an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similarrequest or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, derrial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject petrson.,, (Regulation
1870a.1(a).)
According to your facts, the mayor's business does not qualiff under eith€r of these
standards and is, therefore, indirectly involved in the decision.
5. 
What is the Applicable Materiality Standard?
REAL PROPERTY: If the real property in which an official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standard of
regulation 18705.2(a) applies. (Regulation 187W.2(c)(t).) Regulation 18705.2(a)(t)
provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on real property (other than a
leasehold) that is directly involved in the govemme,ntal decision is piesrrnea to U"
material. "This presumption may be rebutted by proofthat it is not ieasonably foreseeable
that the govemmental decision will have any financial effect on thereal property.,'
(Emphasis added.)
BUSINESS ENTITY: For business entities indirectly involved in governmental
decisions, the effect of a decision is "material" if it reaches the thresholds described in
regulation 18705.1(c). (Regulation 18705.3(bxl).) Thus, for example, if the mayor,s


File No. A-04-192
Page No. 5
business, or a client's business is not publicly traded and is relatively small in size,
pursuant to regulation 18705.1(e)(4), an eflect of a governmental decision is "matelilalu
when:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 ormore; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses orreducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 ormore; or,
'(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000
or more."
6.
fs it Reasonably Foreseeable That the Financial Efrect of the Govenmental
Decision upon Any of Mayor Messina's Economic rnterests wiII Meet the
Applicable Materiality Standard ?
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeablg but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FppC Ops. 198.)
ReaI Property: It is presumed that the financial effect of the decision on the
mayor's property is material. In order to rebut the presumption of regulation
18705.2(a)(1), the mayor is required to show that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have any financial effect on his property. Thus, the mayor would have to
show proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the effect of the project located
within 500 feet of his property will have eve,n a penny's financial effect on his real
property. You have stated that the mayor's business interests will not be affected by an
increase in gross revenue or expenses as a result ofthis decision, and that none of tris
assets and liabilities will be affected by the removal of the tree.
The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. Qn re
Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Therefore, the determination ofwhether or not it is
reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard u.ill be met for the
mayor's real property is necessarily a factual question that is ultimately for the mayor to
decide. In this case, regulation I 8705.2 requires that the mayor provide facts 
"orrrttotirrg
proof supporting a conclusion that the materiality standmd has not been met with respeci
to his real property interest. The Commission requires a good faith effon to be made by
public officials when determining whether a conflict of interest exists. If the mayorhas
utilized a good faith method, such as an appraisal, for determining that it is reasonably
foreseeable that the materiality standard of even a penny's effect on his real property has
not been met, the presumption would be rebutted. While an appraisal is not iequired, the


FileNo. A-04-192
Page No. 6
Mayor's mere belief that there is no financial eflect would not be enough to rebut the
presumption of materiality.
Business: The same standard of a good faith method must be applied to your analysis of
whether there is a financial efi[ect of any kind on the mayor,s business.
In summary, after looking at the methods the mayor has used to evaluate financial effects,
Mayor Messina may then determine if he is able to vote on the decision regarding the two
tree resolutions.
7. & 8. sPublic Generally" and "Legally Required participation, Bxceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies when thereasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effectonthepublicgenerally. (Section 93ll2;regulation lS7O7.) Stepeightisan
exception that applies when the official's participation in a governmenial d-ecision is
legally required. (Section 87101; regulation I S70S.) We have not anallzed these steps.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
"t
By:
Galena
Division
Gwis

resolutions regarding the removal of the eucallptus tree because the business he owns is
within 500 feet from the tree?

CONCLUSION: It is presumed that the financial effect of the tree resolution on Mayor Messina's
real property is material. Therefore, unless the presumption is rebutted by proof that there
is no reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the mayor's properfy, he is presumed to
have a conflict of interest and may not participate in this decision.
